free_text: Fission fragment angular momentum in odd-Z fissioning
systems
Research Reactors CIRUS and APSARA
In the case of fast neutron induced fission in reactor
CIRUS, nitrate solutions of 237 Np (~75 ug) and 241Am
(~50 ug) sealed in polypropylene tubes and covered
with 1 mm thick cadmium foil were irradiated for
3 min to 5 min at a flux 5.0x10**12 n/cm2/sec using
the pneumatic carrier facility of reactor CIRUS.
While in the case of fast neutron induced fission in
reactor APSARA, electrodeposited targets 237Np
(~150 ug) and 241Am (~100 ug) covered with 0.0025 cm
thick superpure aluminium catcher foil and wrapped
with 1 mm thick cadmium foil were irradiated for
20 min to 60 min at a flux 1.2x10**12 n/cm2/sec.
The irradiated solutions or
catcher foils were either for direct gamma ray
spectrometry to make measurements on antimony and
xenon isotopes whereas they were used for
radiochemical separations of tellurium, iodine and
caesium.
Standard aliquots of the separated samples or the
irradiated solution or the aluminium catcher were
analysed gamma ray spectrometrically.
The aluminium catchers or the standard aliquots
of the separated samples were counted at a fixed
geometry on an 80 cm*3 HPGe detector connected to a 4
channel analyser in live-time mode.
The resolution of the detector system was 2.0 keV at
1332.0 keV and the dead time was always less than 10%.
From the photopeak areas of the gamma rays of the
nuclides of interest, independent isomeric yields were
determined using usual decay-growth equations after
correcting for the precursor contribution.
Decay data used in the present work
Decay data used in the present work
The quoted uncertainty on the independent
isomeric yield ratios include the errors due to
counting statistics, absolute abundance of the gamma
lines, detector efficiencies, fission yields of the
precursor and the least square analysis.
